questions.prompt_html:
Solve the problem.

At a plant that packages bottled spring water, the water is passed through a sequence of ion-exchange filters to reduce the sodium content prior to bottling. Each filter removes 70% of the sodium present in the water passing through it. Determine the number of filters that must be used to reduce the sodium concentration from 30 parts-per-million to 7.20 parts-per-million.

questions.prompt_html:
Solve the problem.

A patient takes 100 mg of medication every 24 hours. 80% of the medication in the blood is eliminated every 24 hours.

a. Let dn equal the amount of medication (in mg) in the blood stream after n doses, where d1 = 100. Find a recurrence relation for dn.
b. Show that  is monotonic and bounded, and therefore converges.

questions.prompt_html:
Solve the problem.

A company adopts an advertising campaign to weekly add to its customer base. It assumes that as an average fifty percent of its new customers, those added the previous week, will bring in one friend, but those who have been customers longer will not be very effective as recruiters and can be discounted. A media campaign brings in 10,000 customers initially. What is the expected total number of customers with whom the company can expect to have dealings?

A company makes a very durable product. How many products can the company expect to eventually sell?

Solve the problem.

A child on a swing initially swings through an arc length of 12 meters. The child stops pushing and sits patiently waiting for the swing to stop moving. If friction slows the swing so the length of each arc is 80% of the length of the previous arc, how far will the child have traveled before the swing stops?

choices.label: C

(ii)  ≈ P2(- 0.82) = 1.0564, and the absolute error satisfies |E2(-0.82)| < 0.00036.

questions.prompt_html:
A function f(x) satisfies the following conditions:

f(1) = 0,f(n)(1) = n for n = 1, 2, and 3,
 3x ≤ f(4)(x) ≤ 9x provided x ≥ 1.

(a)What is the Taylor polynomial P3(x) of degree 3 for f(x) about x = 1?
(b)What is the approximate value for f(3/2) supplied by P3(x)?
(c)Based on the bounds for f(4)(x) given above, what is the smallest interval that you can be sure
 contains the value f(3/2)?
(d)Based on your answer to (c), what is the best approximation you can give for f(3/2)?
questions.prompt_text: A function f(x) satisfies the following conditions: f(1) = 0, f (n) (1) = n for n = 1, 2, and 3, 3x ≤ f (4) (x) ≤ 9x provided x ≥ 1. (a) What is the Taylor polynomial P 3 (x) of degree 3 for f(x) about x = 1? (b) What is the approximate value for f(3/2) supplied by P 3 (x)? (c) Based on the bounds for f (4) (x) given above, what is the smallest interval that you can be sure contains the value f(3/2)? (d) Based on your answer to (c), what is the best approximation you can give for f(3/2)?

(a) P3(x) = x - 1 + (x - 1)2 +  (x - 1)3
(b) f(3/2) ≈ 13/16
(c) f(3/2) ∈ [105/128, 217/256] ≈ [0.820, 0.848] 
(d) f(3/2) ≈  ≈ 0.834

questions.prompt_html:
Show that the infinite series  converges, and find its sum.
Hint: Verify that tan-1 = tan-1 - tan-1 .
questions.prompt_text: Show that the infinite series sum of ((tan) with superscript (-1)((1/(2n) with superscript (2)))) from (n=1) to (∞) converges, and find its sum. Hint: Verify that tan -1 ((1/(2n) with superscript (2))) = tan -1 ((1/2n - 1)) - tan -1 ((1/2n + 1)) .
questions.solution_html:
Let α = tan-1 and β = tan-1 . Therefore tan(α) =  and tan(β) = .
Recall the identity tan (α - β) =  =  =  = 
It follows that α - β = tan-1, that is 
 tan-1 - tan-1 = tan-1 (verified) ..........(*)
Now if we let an = tan-1 and bn = tan-1 (and hence bn +1 = tan-1 )
we can write (*) in the compact form an = bn - bn + 1, n = 1, 2, 3, ...
So, the given series is in fact a Telescoping Series!
The nth partial sum sn is thus given by :
sn =a1 + a2 + ....+ an = (b1 - b2) + (b2 - b3)) + ......+ ( bn - bn + 1). Hence 
 sn = b1 - bn + 1 = tan-1(1) - tan-1 =  - tan-1 
It follows that sn =  =  - 0 = 
The series converges, and its sum is .
questions.solution_text: Let α = tan -1 ((1/2n - 1)) and β = tan -1 ((1/2n + 1)) . Therefore tan(α) = (1/2n - 1) and tan(β) = (1/2n + 1) . Recall the identity tan (α - β) = (tan(α) - tan(β)/1 + tan(α)tan(β) ) = ((1/2n -1) - (1/2n +1)/1 + (1/2n - 1). (1/2n + 1)) = (2/4(n) with superscript (2)) = (1/2(n) with superscript (2)) It follows that α - β = tan -1 ((1/2(n) with superscript (2))) , that is tan -1 ((1/2n - 1)) - tan -1 ((1/2n + 1)) = tan -1 ((1/2(n) with superscript (2))) (verified) ..........(*) Now if we let a n = tan -1 ((1/2(n) with superscript (2))) and b n = tan -1 ((1/2n -1 )) (and hence b n +1 = tan -1 ((1/2n +1 )) ) we can write (*) in the compact form a n = b n - b n + 1 , n = 1, 2, 3, ... So, the given series is in fact a Telescoping Series! The nth partial sum s n is thus given by : s n =a 1 + a 2 + ....+ a n = (b 1 - b 2 ) + (b 2 - b 3) ) + ......+ ( b n - b n + 1 ). Hence s n = b 1 - b n + 1 = tan -1 (1) - tan -1 ((1/2n +1 )) = (π/4) - tan -1 ((1/2n +1 )) It follows that (n → ∞) is under (lim) s n = (n → ∞) is under (lim) ( (π/4) - (tan) with superscript (-1)((1/2n + 1 )) ) = (π/4) - 0 = (π/4) The series converges, and its sum is (π/4) .

questions.solution_text: From the graph (shown below), it is immediately evident that f is an odd function on the symmetric interval (- π, π). Short Description: A graph shows two lines. Long Description: The x axis, t ranges from negative 5 to 5 in increments of 1 and the y axis, f of t ranges from negative 5 to 5 in increments of 1. The first line slopes downward from (0, 3) to (3, 0). The second line slopes downward from (negative 3, 0) to (0, negative 3). Therefore, f has a Fourier sine series sum of ((b) with subscript (n)) from (n =1) to (∞) sin(nωt), where b n = (2/L) integral of (f(t)sin(nωt) dt) from (0) to (L) . Here L = half the period = (2π/2) = π and ω = (π/L) = (π/π) = 1. Now let us compute the Fourier coefficients b n , n = 1,2, 3, .... Integrating by parts, we obtain: b n = (2/π) integral of ((π -t) sin(nt)) from (0) to (π) dt = (2/n) Therefore the Fourier sine series is given by sum of ((2sin(nt)/n)) from (n =1) to (∞) .
